docs.title: WordBase
docs.text:
A lightweight Python wrapper for accessing LLMs from multiple sources — HuggingFace (local or hosted), OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Amazon Bedrock, and vLLM — through one unified interface.

  • Unified interface — the same generate()/generate_batch() contract across every provider
  • Token-level information — log-probabilities per token, when the provider supports it
  • Built-in caching — in-memory, SQLite, or one-JSON-file-per-entry, opt out per request with use_cache=False
  • Batch processing — true padded batching locally (HuggingFace/vLLM), concurrent requests for API providers
  • Classification primitives — turn any provider into a calibrated label distribution via LLMDistribution/classify(), without hand-rolling prompt-to-logprob plumbing
  • Multimodal (text + image) input — supported for HuggingFace, OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, and Amazon Bedrock; not yet supported for vLLM or the HuggingFace Inference API

docs.title: Where to go next
docs.text:
• :material-download: Installation Pick the provider extras you need.

  • :material-rocket-launch: Quickstart Your first generate() call in a few lines.

  • :material-book-open-variant: Guides One page per provider, plus caching and classification.

  • :material-api: API Reference Generated straight from the docstrings, starting at LLMProvider.

docs.title: A minimal example
docs.text:
from wordbase import HuggingFaceProvider, Message

provider = HuggingFaceProvider(model_name="Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct")

messages = [
    Message(role="system", content="You are a helpful assistant."),
    Message(role="user", content="What is the capital of France?"),
]

completions = provider.generate(messages, temperature=0.7, max_tokens=100)
print(completions[0]["text"])

Every provider implements the same generate() signature, so swapping HuggingFaceProvider for OpenAIProvider(model="gpt-4o") or AnthropicProvider() changes nothing else about the call above (beyond needing that provider's API key).

Runnable, fully-worked examples for every provider live in examples/ in the repository; narrative tutorial notebooks that run against a local model with no API keys required live in notebooks/.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: WordBase follows a strict Google Python Style Guide + test-driven workflow. The authoritative reference for contributors is CLAUDE.md in the repository root — this page is a short pointer, not a duplicate.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text: poetry install -E dev -E all      # dev tooling + every provider extra

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. Write a failing test first (tests/test_*.py)
  2. Implement the feature
  3. poetry run black src/ tests/ — format
  4. poetry run pylint src/wordbase tests/ — must pass with zero errors
  5. poetry run mypy src/wordbase — must pass with zero errors
  6. poetry run pytest -m "not integration" — fast unit suite; coverage must stay ≥95%

A pre-commit hook (.git/hooks/pre-commit) runs black, pylint, mypy, and ruff automatically before every commit.

docs.title: Adding a new provider
docs.text: See CLAUDE.md's "Adding a New Provider" section for the full checklist — in short: subclass APIProvider for a cloud API or LLMProvider directly for a local inference engine, add an optional-dependency extra in pyproject.toml, and add coverage to tests/test_integration.py.

docs.title: Building this documentation site locally
docs.text:
poetry install -E docs
poetry run mkdocs serve   # live-reloading preview at http://127.0.0.1:8000

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
WordBase uses Poetry and installs providers as opt-in extras, so you only pull in the SDKs you actually use.

poetry install                    # core only, no provider SDKs
poetry install -E huggingface     # local/hosted HuggingFace models (transformers, torch)
poetry install -E openai          # OpenAI
poetry install -E anthropic       # Anthropic
poetry install -E google          # Google Gemini
poetry install -E bedrock         # Amazon Bedrock
poetry install -E hf-inference    # HuggingFace Inference API (serverless or dedicated endpoints)
poetry install -E vllm            # vLLM (high-throughput local inference; requires CUDA)
poetry install -E classification  # LLMDistribution / classify() / surface_logprobs()
poetry install -E calibration     # + Platt-scaling / temperature-scaling calibration
poetry install -E all             # everything above, including vllm (still needs a CUDA GPU to run)

Extras compose, so poetry install -E huggingface -E openai -E classification installs exactly what you need.

docs.title: Provider credentials
docs.text:
Provider Class Needs HuggingFace (local/hosted) HuggingFaceProvider -E huggingface; downloads model weights on first use vLLM (high-throughput local) VLLMProvider -E vllm; requires a CUDA GPU OpenAI OpenAIProvider -E openai; OPENAI_API_KEY Anthropic AnthropicProvider -E anthropic; ANTHROPIC_API_KEY Google Gemini GoogleProvider -E google; GOOGLE_API_KEY Amazon Bedrock BedrockProvider -E bedrock; AWS credentials (env vars, ~/.aws/credentials, or IAM role) HuggingFace Inference API HuggingFaceInferenceProvider -E hf-inference; HF_TOKEN

Each API key can also be passed explicitly via the provider's api_key= constructor argument instead of an environment variable.

Next: Quickstart.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: This walks through the core generate() call using a free, local model — no API keys needed. See the guides for the same workflow against a hosted API.

docs.title: First call
docs.text:
from wordbase import HuggingFaceProvider, Message

provider = HuggingFaceProvider(model_name="Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct")

messages = [Message(role="user", content="What is the capital of France?")]
completions = provider.generate(messages, temperature=0.7, max_tokens=100)

print(completions[0]["text"])

The first call downloads the model (~1 GB) and caches it under ~/.cache/huggingface/; subsequent runs reuse the cached weights.

docs.title: The completion dict
docs.text:
Every generate() call returns a list of completion dicts (length n, default 1):

completion = completions[0]
completion["text"]           # str: the generated text
completion["finish_reason"]  # str: why generation stopped ("stop", "length", ...)
completion["tokens"]         # list[dict]: per-token {"text", "logprob", "token_id"}, only when return_token_probs=True
completion["metadata"]       # dict: provider-specific info (token counts, model name, ...)

docs.title: Multi-turn conversation
docs.text:
messages = [
    Message(role="system", content="You are a helpful assistant."),
    Message(role="user", content="Hello!"),
    Message(role="assistant", content="Hi there! How can I help?"),
    Message(role="user", content="What's 2 + 2?"),
]
completions = provider.generate(messages, temperature=0.0, max_tokens=20)

docs.title: Switching providers
docs.text:
Every provider implements the same generate()/generate_batch() contract, so switching from a local model to a hosted API is a one-line change:

from wordbase import OpenAIProvider

provider = OpenAIProvider(model="gpt-4o")  # reads OPENAI_API_KEY from the environment

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Caching — avoid re-paying for identical requests
  • Classification — turn a provider into a calibrated label distribution
  • One guide per provider, starting with HuggingFace
  • The notebooks/ directory has narrative, runnable walkthroughs of all of the above

docs.title: Anthropic
docs.text:
poetry install -E anthropic

from wordbase import AnthropicProvider, Message

provider = AnthropicProvider(model="claude-sonnet-4-6")  # or api_key="sk-ant-..." explicitly

Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment by default.

docs.title: What's native vs. emulated
docs.text:
• n completions: emulated — Anthropic has no native n, so n concurrent requests are issued via a thread pool.
  • Token counting: a real API call to Anthropic's count_tokens endpoint (not free/local, unlike OpenAI's tiktoken).
  • Token-level logprobs: not available through the Anthropic API — completion["tokens"] is always empty regardless of return_token_probs.
  • Extended thinking: AnthropicProvider surfaces a response's thinking content under completion["thinking"] when the API returns one, but does not yet expose a parameter to request thinking mode on the call itself.

docs.title: Multimodal (vision) input
docs.text:
Claude's vision-capable models accept image content blocks directly — no separate constructor flag needed:

from wordbase import AnthropicProvider, Message
from wordbase.types import ImageContent, TextContent

provider = AnthropicProvider(model="claude-sonnet-4-6")

messages = [
    Message(
        role="user",
        content=[
            TextContent(type="text", text="What's in this image?"),
            ImageContent(type="image", source="path/to/photo.jpg"),
        ],
    )
]
completions = provider.generate(messages, max_tokens=100)

ImageContent.source accepts an http(s):// URL (fetched server-side by Anthropic), a data URI, or a local file path — file paths and data URIs are base64-encoded automatically.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• examples/anthropic_provider.py — generation, token counting, n>1, caching
  • API reference

docs.title: Amazon Bedrock
docs.text:
poetry install -E bedrock

from wordbase import BedrockProvider, Message

provider = BedrockProvider(model="anthropic.claude-3-haiku-20240307-v1:0", region="us-east-1")

AWS credentials are resolved in order: explicit constructor arguments (aws_access_key_id, aws_secret_access_key, aws_session_token) → AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID/AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY/AWS_SESSION_TOKEN environment variables → ~/.aws/credentials → IAM instance role. Model access must be enabled for the model you use in the Bedrock console's "Model access" page.

docs.title: What's native vs. emulated
docs.text:
• n completions: emulated — Bedrock's converse API has no native n, so n concurrent requests are issued via a thread pool.
  • Token counting: a character-based estimate (~4 characters/token) with a UserWarning — the converse API has no dedicated token-counting endpoint.
  • Token-level logprobs: not available through the converse API — completion["tokens"] is always empty regardless of return_token_probs.
  • Switching model IDs: the same provider interface works against any Bedrock-hosted model — just change the model= string (e.g. Anthropic, Meta Llama, Amazon Titan/Nova).

docs.title: Multimodal (vision) input
docs.text:
Vision-capable model IDs (e.g. Claude on Bedrock) accept image content blocks directly — no separate constructor flag needed:

from wordbase import BedrockProvider, Message
from wordbase.types import ImageContent, TextContent

provider = BedrockProvider(model="anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-6")

messages = [
    Message(
        role="user",
        content=[
            TextContent(type="text", text="What's in this image?"),
            ImageContent(type="image", source="path/to/photo.jpg"),
        ],
    )
]
completions = provider.generate(messages, max_tokens=100)

ImageContent.source accepts a local file path or a data URI — both are loaded as raw bytes for converse's image content block (unlike OpenAI/Anthropic/Google, converse wants raw bytes rather than a base64 string; boto3 handles the wire encoding). A bare http(s):// URL is not supported and raises InvalidRequestError — download the image yourself first. BedrockProvider does not maintain its own allowlist of vision-capable model IDs; passing image content to a model that doesn't support it surfaces as InvalidRequestError via converse's own ValidationException.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• examples/bedrock_provider.py — generation, token counting, switching model IDs, n>1, caching
  • API reference

docs.title: Caching
docs.text:
Every provider supports caching through the same interface: attach a Cache instance with set_cache(), and identical requests are served from the cache instead of making a real call.

from wordbase import HuggingFaceProvider, MemoryCache, Message

provider = HuggingFaceProvider(model_name="Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct")
provider.set_cache(MemoryCache())

messages = [Message(role="user", content="What is Planck's constant?")]
provider.generate(messages, temperature=0.0)   # miss: real call, then stored
provider.generate(messages, temperature=0.0)   # hit: returned from cache, no call

Cache keys are SHA-256 hashes of the canonicalized request (messages + every generation parameter), so any change to the request — a different message, a different temperature, etc. — produces a different key and a cache miss.

docs.title: Per-request override
docs.text: provider.generate(messages, use_cache=False)   # always makes a real call, bypassing the cache

docs.title: clear_cache()
docs.text: provider.clear_cache()   # empties the cache; the next identical request recomputes

docs.title: Choosing an implementation
docs.text:
Cache Use when MemoryCache Testing or short-lived scripts — no persistence, gone when the process exits SQLiteCache Production workloads — single file, atomic writes, thread-safe, any dataset size FileCache Debugging — one human-readable JSON file per entry, easy to inspect or delete individually 
from wordbase import SQLiteCache, FileCache

cache = SQLiteCache("~/.cache/wordbase/cache.db", default_ttl=3600)
# or:
cache = FileCache("~/.cache/wordbase/entries/", default_ttl=3600)

provider.set_cache(cache)

docs.title: Batch requests
docs.text: generate_batch() respects the cache the same way generate() does — each request in the batch is checked individually, so a batch can be a mix of cache hits and misses.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• examples/huggingface_batch.py — caching with MemoryCache, SQLiteCache, and FileCache
  • notebooks/02_caching.ipynb — narrative walkthrough with timing comparisons
  • API reference

docs.title: Classification & LLMDistribution
docs.text:
WordBase can turn any provider into a calibrated label classifier, without hand-rolling the prompt-to-logprob plumbing yourself. This works with any provider — including ones with no native logprob support, via a sampling-based fallback.

poetry install -E classification   # LLMDistribution / classify() / surface_logprobs()
poetry install -E calibration      # + Platt/temperature-scaling calibration fitting

docs.title: One-shot: classify()
docs.text:
The quickest path — no object to construct, no Verbalizer to define if your labels are already the surface forms you want to detect:

from wordbase import HuggingFaceProvider, classify

provider = HuggingFaceProvider("Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct")

probs = classify(
    provider,
    "Is the sky blue? Answer yes or no.",
    labels=["yes", "no"],
)
# {"yes": -0.05, "no": -2.93}  -- see the units note below

Units: log-probabilities

classify() and LLMDistribution.query() return log-probabilities (values <= 0, exp() of them sums to ~1 across labels) — deliberately, on every backend path, including SamplingBackend (whose posterior mean is a linear probability internally, log-transformed before returning). Log-space is more numerically stable than renormalizing linear probabilities, particularly for calibration's temperature/Platt scaling. math.exp() converts a value to a human-readable probability when you want one.

docs.title: Inspecting raw scores: surface_logprobs()
docs.text:
Useful for seeing what the model assigns to candidate strings before committing to a full classification setup:

from wordbase import surface_logprobs

raw = surface_logprobs(provider, "Is the sky blue? Answer yes or no.", surfaces=["yes", "no"])
# {"yes": -9.19, "no": -12.06}  (raw, unnormalized log-probabilities -- not
# comparable to classify()'s output above, which aggregates and renormalizes
# across the full surface set via the Verbalizer)

docs.title: Reusable: LLMDistribution
docs.text:
For repeated classification with the same prompt template, verbalizer, and calibration, build an LLMDistribution once and call query()/query_batch() many times:

from wordbase import LLMDistribution, BOOL_VERBALIZER

dist = LLMDistribution(
    provider,
    verbalizer=BOOL_VERBALIZER,
    prompt_template="Is this statement true? {input}\nAnswer yes or no.",
)

result = dist.query("The Earth orbits the Sun.")
# {True: -0.20, False: -1.72}  -- log-probabilities; see the units note above

results = dist.query_batch([
    "The Earth orbits the Sun.",
    "The Moon is made of cheese.",
])

Verbalizer maps each label to one or more surface forms it should match (BOOL_VERBALIZER and SENTIMENT_VERBALIZER ship as ready-made verbalizers). Build your own with Verbalizer({label: [surface_forms...]}), or Verbalizer.identity(labels) when each label is already its own surface form.

docs.title: How the backend is chosen
docs.text:
LLMDistribution/classify() auto-select the most capable backend for the given provider:

  • HFLogitBackend — HuggingFace providers with a real forward pass: exact per-label log-probabilities in one call, no sampling needed.
  • HFClassifierBackend — HuggingFaceClassifierProvider (a sequence-classification head): the model's own label distribution, no Verbalizer needed.
  • APILogprobBackend — API providers that expose top-k token log-probabilities (OpenAI): scores surface forms from the returned logprobs.
  • SamplingBackend — everything else (Anthropic, Google, Bedrock, HF Inference API, or any provider without logprob access): draws repeated samples and estimates the label distribution from the sample counts, with adaptive stopping once a target precision is reached.

You can also pass an explicit backend= to LLMDistribution to override auto-selection.

docs.title: Calibration
docs.text:
Raw model confidence is often over- or under-confident. Fit a calibration transform from held-out labeled examples:

dist.fit_calibration(
    texts=["The Earth orbits the Sun.", "The Moon is made of cheese."],
    labels=[True, False],
    method="platt",   # or "temperature"
)
result = dist.query("Water boils at 100°C at sea level.")  # now calibrated

method="temperature" needs only scipy (the classification extra); method="platt" additionally needs scikit-learn (the calibration extra).

docs.title: Gradient workflows
docs.text: query(..., return_tensors=True) returns log-probabilities as PyTorch tensors with an attached computation graph — only supported on the HFLogitBackend path (a real local forward pass), and raises NotImplementedError on the sampling or classifier paths.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• notebooks/03_classification.ipynb — full narrative walkthrough
  • API reference: LLMDistribution, classify()/surface_logprobs(), Verbalizer, Calibration

docs.title: Google Gemini
docs.text:
poetry install -E google

from wordbase import GoogleProvider, Message

provider = GoogleProvider(model="gemini-2.0-flash")  # or api_key="..." explicitly

Reads GOOGLE_API_KEY from the environment by default. Uses the Gemini Developer API via the google-genai SDK.

docs.title: What's native vs. emulated
docs.text:
• n completions: emulated — Gemini has no native n for this API, so n concurrent requests are issued via a thread pool.
  • Token counting: a real API call to Gemini's count_tokens endpoint.
  • Token-level logprobs: not available through the standard Gemini API — completion["tokens"] is always empty regardless of return_token_probs.
  • System prompts: passed as a separate system_instruction parameter rather than a message, and the assistant role is mapped to Gemini's "model" role internally — handled automatically by _format_messages().

docs.title: Multimodal (vision) input
docs.text:
Gemini models are natively multimodal — no separate constructor flag needed:

from wordbase import GoogleProvider, Message
from wordbase.types import ImageContent, TextContent

provider = GoogleProvider(model="gemini-2.0-flash")

messages = [
    Message(
        role="user",
        content=[
            TextContent(type="text", text="What's in this image?"),
            ImageContent(type="image", source="path/to/photo.jpg"),
        ],
    )
]
completions = provider.generate(messages, max_tokens=100)

ImageContent.source accepts a local file path or a data URI — both are sent as an inline base64 inline_data part. Unlike OpenAI/Anthropic, a bare http(s):// URL is not supported: Gemini's inline_data part requires the actual image bytes rather than fetching a URL server-side, so passing one raises InvalidRequestError. Download the image yourself first if you only have a URL.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• examples/google_provider.py — generation, token counting, n>1, caching
  • API reference

docs.title: HuggingFace Inference API
docs.text:
HuggingFaceInferenceProvider uses InferenceClient's chat_completion() endpoint against either the Hub's serverless inference or a dedicated Inference Endpoint — no local GPU or model download required.

poetry install -E hf-inference

from wordbase import HuggingFaceInferenceProvider, Message

provider = HuggingFaceInferenceProvider(model="meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct")

Reads HF_TOKEN from the environment by default.

docs.title: Dedicated endpoints
docs.text:
Point at a deployed Inference Endpoint instead of serverless inference by passing endpoint_url=; the model ID is then used only for metadata:

provider = HuggingFaceInferenceProvider(
    model="your-org/your-model",
    endpoint_url="https://your-endpoint-id.endpoints.huggingface.cloud",
)

docs.title: What's native vs. emulated
docs.text:
• n completions: emulated — the HF Inference API has no native n, so n concurrent requests are issued via a thread pool.
  • Token counting: lazy-loads a local transformers tokenizer for the model on first use; falls back to a character-based estimate (with a UserWarning) if that fails or transformers isn't installed.
  • Token-level logprobs: available for models that expose them; when unavailable a UserWarning is emitted and completion["tokens"] is left empty.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• examples/huggingface_inference_provider.py — generation, token counting, logprobs, n>1, dedicated endpoints, caching
  • API reference

docs.title: HuggingFace (local/hosted)
docs.text:
HuggingFaceProvider runs models locally (or from a local path) via transformers. No API key needed — the first call downloads the model weights.

poetry install -E huggingface

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
from wordbase import HuggingFaceProvider, Message

provider = HuggingFaceProvider(model_name="Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct", device="auto")

messages = [Message(role="user", content="Hello!")]
completions = provider.generate(messages, temperature=0.7, max_tokens=100)

device accepts "auto", "cuda", "cpu", or "mps". load_in_8bit=True enables 8-bit quantization to reduce memory usage.

docs.title: Sampling strategies
docs.text: All the standard knobs are supported: temperature, top_p, top_k, and num_beams (beam search — the only provider here that supports it; API providers don't).

docs.title: Token-level log-probabilities
docs.text:
completions = provider.generate(messages, return_token_probs=True, max_tokens=20)
for tok in completions[0]["tokens"]:
    print(tok["text"], tok["logprob"])

docs.title: True batching
docs.text:
generate_batch() groups requests by generation parameters and runs each group as a single padded batch through the model — this is real batching, not just concurrent calls:

batch = [
    {"messages": [Message(role="user", content="Q1")]},
    {"messages": [Message(role="user", content="Q2")]},
]
results = provider.generate_batch(batch, max_tokens=50)

docs.title: Gradient workflows
docs.text: generate(..., return_tensors=True) returns logprobs as PyTorch tensors with an attached computation graph, so you can call .backward() directly on the result — see examples/gradient_optimization.py. Caching is automatically disabled in this mode since gradient-carrying tensors can't be cached.

docs.title: Multimodal (vision) input
docs.text:
Pass multimodal=True to load a vision-language model (AutoProcessor + AutoModelForImageTextToText) instead of the default text-only AutoTokenizer + AutoModelForCausalLM:

from wordbase import HuggingFaceProvider, Message
from wordbase.types import ImageContent, TextContent

provider = HuggingFaceProvider(model_name="llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf", multimodal=True)

messages = [
    Message(
        role="user",
        content=[
            TextContent(type="text", text="What's in this image?"),
            ImageContent(type="image", source="path/to/photo.jpg"),
        ],
    )
]
completions = provider.generate(messages, max_tokens=100)

ImageContent.source accepts a local file path or a base64 data URI. Calling generate()/generate_batch() with image content on a provider constructed without multimodal=True raises InvalidRequestError.

generate_batch() routes any request containing images through generate() individually rather than the padded true-batching path — true-batching assumes a uniform shape per request, which breaks down once requests carry a varying number of images. Text-only requests in the same batch still use the efficient grouped sub-batch path.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• examples/huggingface_basic.py, huggingface_conversation.py, huggingface_token_probs.py, huggingface_batch.py
  • API reference

docs.title: OpenAI
docs.text:
poetry install -E openai

from wordbase import OpenAIProvider, Message

provider = OpenAIProvider(model="gpt-4o")  # or api_key="sk-..." explicitly

Reads OPENAI_API_KEY from the environment by default. organization= and base_url= (e.g. for Azure OpenAI) are also accepted.

docs.title: What's native vs. emulated
docs.text:
• n completions: native — one API call returns all n choices.
  • Token counting: local, via tiktoken — no API call.
  • Token-level logprobs: supported via return_token_probs=True; top_logprobs=N requests the top-N alternatives per position.
  • Batching: generate_batch() issues concurrent requests via a thread pool (OpenAI has no server-side batch endpoint used here).
completions = provider.generate(messages, n=3, return_token_probs=True, max_tokens=60)

docs.title: Multimodal (vision) input
docs.text:
Any GPT-4o-class vision-capable model accepts image content blocks directly — no separate constructor flag needed, since the format lives entirely in the request payload:

from wordbase import OpenAIProvider, Message
from wordbase.types import ImageContent, TextContent

provider = OpenAIProvider(model="gpt-4o")

messages = [
    Message(
        role="user",
        content=[
            TextContent(type="text", text="What's in this image?"),
            ImageContent(type="image", source="https://example.com/photo.jpg"),
        ],
    )
]
completions = provider.generate(messages, max_tokens=100)

ImageContent.source accepts an http(s):// URL, a data URI, or a local file path — file paths are loaded and base64-encoded automatically.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• examples/openai_provider.py — full walkthrough: generation, token counting, n>1, logprobs, batching, caching
  • API reference

docs.title: vLLM
docs.text:
VLLMProvider runs HuggingFace-compatible models through vLLM's PagedAttention and continuous-batching engine — much higher throughput than HuggingFaceProvider for serving workloads, at the cost of requiring a CUDA GPU.

poetry install -E vllm

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
from wordbase import VLLMProvider, Message

provider = VLLMProvider(model_name="meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf", tensor_parallel_size=1)

messages = [Message(role="user", content="Hello!")]
completions = provider.generate(messages, temperature=0.7, max_tokens=100)

Extra keyword arguments (tensor_parallel_size, dtype, gpu_memory_utilization, max_model_len, ...) are forwarded straight to vllm.LLM().

docs.title: Batching
docs.text:
generate_batch() groups requests by generation parameters and submits each group together to vLLM's engine, which handles scheduling and KV-cache management internally — this is where vLLM's throughput advantage shows up:

batch = [{"messages": [Message(role="user", content=q)]} for q in questions]
results = provider.generate_batch(batch, max_tokens=100)

docs.title: Token-level log-probabilities
docs.text:
completions = provider.generate(messages, return_token_probs=True, max_tokens=20)

Extracted via SamplingParams.logprobs; values are negative log-probabilities, as expected.

docs.title: Unit tests without a GPU
docs.text: tests/test_vllm_provider.py and tests/test_integration.py mock vllm via sys.modules injection, so the unit test suite runs on any machine. Real-GPU tests are separately marked @pytest.mark.vllm_integration and excluded from the default CI run.

docs.text:

  • examples/benchmark_providers.py — HuggingFace vs. vLLM wall-clock throughput
  • API reference

docs.title: wordbase.cache
docs.text:
Caching interface and implementations for WordBase.

This module provides an abstract Cache interface and concrete implementations for caching LLM responses. Caching helps reduce API costs and latency by storing and reusing previous responses for identical requests.

The module includes
  • Cache: Abstract base class defining the caching interface
  • DiskCache: Abstract base class for persistent disk-based caches
  • MemoryCache: In-memory cache with TTL support
  • SQLiteCache: Persistent cache using SQLite with JSON-encoded values
  • FileCache: Persistent cache using one JSON file per entry
  • Utility functions for generating cache keys from requests

Typical usage example:

from wordbase.cache import MemoryCache, SQLiteCache
from wordbase.types import Message

# In-memory cache (fast, not persistent)
cache = MemoryCache()

# Persistent SQLite cache (recommended for disk caching)
cache = SQLiteCache("~/.cache/wordbase/cache.db")

messages = [Message(role="user", content="Hello")]

# Generate cache key
key = cache.make_key(messages, system_prompt=None, temperature=0.7)

# Store and retrieve
cache.set(key, {"text": "Response"}, ttl=3600)
result = cache.get(key)

@staticmethod
def make_key(
    messages: list["Message"], system_prompt: str | None, **params: Any
) -> str:
    """Generate cache key from request parameters.

    Creates a hash of the canonicalized request to ensure consistent
    keys for identical requests regardless of parameter order.

    Args:
        messages: Conversation history as Message objects.
        system_prompt: System prompt.
        **params: Additional generation parameters.

    Returns:
        SHA-256 hash of the canonicalized request.
    """
    # Convert Message objects to dicts for serialization
    messages_dicts = [msg.to_dict() for msg in messages]

    # Canonicalize request
    request = {"messages": messages_dicts, "system_prompt": system_prompt, **params}
    # Sort keys for consistent hashing
    canonical = json.dumps(request, sort_keys=True)
    return hashlib.sha256(canonical.encode()).hexdigest()

Abstract base class for persistent disk-based cache implementations.

DiskCache extends the Cache interface with additional methods for managing persistent storage, including cleanup of expired entries and size tracking.

def get(self, key: str) -> Any | None:
    """Retrieve cached value by key.

    Args:
        key: Cache key.

    Returns:
        Cached value if found and not expired, None otherwise.
    """
    if key not in self._cache:
        return None

    value, expiry = self._cache[key]

    # Check expiration
    if expiry is not None and time.time() > expiry:
        # Entry has expired, remove it
        del self._cache[key]
        return None

    return value

Bases: DiskCache

Persistent cache using SQLite database with JSON-encoded values.

SQLiteCache stores cache entries in a SQLite database file, providing persistence across sessions and built-in concurrent access safety. Values are JSON-encoded for human readability and cross-platform compatibility.

Attributes:

default_ttl

Default time-to-live in seconds for entries.

_conn

SQLite database connection.

_lock

Threading lock for thread-safe access.

Initialize the SQLite cache.

Creates the database file and parent directories if they don't exist. Optionally cleans up expired entries on initialization.

Note: Does not call super().init() because the database connection must be established before cleanup_expired() can be called.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default path str

Path to the SQLite database file.

required default_ttl int | None

Default TTL in seconds for entries without explicit TTL. None means no expiration by default.

None cleanup_on_init bool

True Source code in src/wordbase/cache.py 
def __init__(
    self, path: str, default_ttl: int | None = None, cleanup_on_init: bool = True
) -> None:
    """Initialize the SQLite cache.

    Creates the database file and parent directories if they don't exist.
    Optionally cleans up expired entries on initialization.

    Note: Does not call super().__init__() because the database connection
    must be established before cleanup_expired() can be called.

    Args:
        path: Path to the SQLite database file.
        default_ttl: Default TTL in seconds for entries without explicit
            TTL. None means no expiration by default.
        cleanup_on_init: Whether to clean up expired entries on
            initialization. Defaults to True.
    """
    self.path = path
    self.default_ttl = default_ttl
    self._lock = threading.Lock()

    # Create parent directories if needed
    db_path = Path(path)
    db_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    # Initialize database connection with timeout for concurrent access
    self._conn = sqlite3.connect(path, timeout=30.0, check_same_thread=False)

    # Enable WAL mode for better concurrent access
    self._conn.execute("PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL")

    self._create_schema()

    # Cleanup after connection is established
    if cleanup_on_init:
        self.cleanup_expired()

def get(self, key: str) -> Any | None:
    """Retrieve cached value by key.

    If the entry is expired, it is deleted and None is returned.
    Handles corrupted JSON values gracefully by returning None.

    Args:
        key: Cache key.

    Returns:
        Cached value if found and not expired, None otherwise.
    """
    with self._lock:
        cursor = self._conn.execute(
            "SELECT value, expires_at FROM cache WHERE key = ?", (key,)
        )
        row = cursor.fetchone()

        if row is None:
            return None

        value_json, expires_at = row

        # Check expiration
        if expires_at is not None and time.time() > expires_at:
            self._conn.execute("DELETE FROM cache WHERE key = ?", (key,))
            self._conn.commit()
            return None

        # Decode JSON, handle corrupted values
        try:
            return json.loads(value_json)
        except (json.JSONDecodeError, TypeError):
            # Corrupted value, remove it
            self._conn.execute("DELETE FROM cache WHERE key = ?", (key,))
            self._conn.commit()
            return None

def set(self, key: str, value: Any, ttl: int | None = None) -> None:
    """Store value in cache.

    Args:
        key: Cache key.
        value: Value to cache (must be JSON-serializable).
        ttl: Time-to-live in seconds. If None, uses default_ttl.
            If both are None, entry never expires.
    """
    # Use explicit TTL, fall back to default
    effective_ttl = ttl if ttl is not None else self.default_ttl
    expires_at = time.time() + effective_ttl if effective_ttl else None

    value_json = json.dumps(value)

    with self._lock:
        self._conn.execute(
            "INSERT OR REPLACE INTO cache (key, value, expires_at) "
            "VALUES (?, ?, ?)",
            (key, value_json, expires_at),
        )
        self._conn.commit()

FileCache(path: str, default_ttl: int | None = None, cleanup_on_init: bool = True)

Bases: DiskCache

Persistent cache using flat JSON files, one file per entry.

FileCache stores each cache entry as a JSON file inside a directory. The filename is derived from a SHA-256 hash of the cache key, so any key string maps to a valid, filesystem-safe filename.

Best suited for debugging and inspection workflows where human-readable cache files are desirable. For production workloads, SQLiteCache is recommended for better performance and atomic write semantics.

def __init__(
    self,
    path: str,
    default_ttl: int | None = None,
    cleanup_on_init: bool = True,
) -> None:
    """Initialize the file cache.

    Creates the cache directory (and any missing parents). Optionally
    removes expired entries on startup.

    Note: Does not call super().__init__() because the directory must
    be created before cleanup_expired() can run.

    Args:
        path: Path to the cache directory.
        default_ttl: Default TTL in seconds for entries without an
            explicit TTL. None means entries never expire by default.
        cleanup_on_init: Whether to delete expired files on
            initialization. Defaults to True.
    """
    self.path = path
    self.default_ttl = default_ttl
    self._lock = threading.Lock()

    Path(path).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    if cleanup_on_init:
        self.cleanup_expired()

def get(self, key: str) -> Any | None:
    """Retrieve a cached value by key.

    Reads the corresponding JSON file. Returns None if the file does
    not exist, is corrupted, or the entry has expired. Expired and
    corrupted files are deleted on access.

    Args:
        key: Cache key.

    Returns:
        Cached value if found and not expired, None otherwise.
    """
    file_path = self._key_to_path(key)
    with self._lock:
        if not file_path.exists():
            return None
        try:
            data = json.loads(file_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
        except (json.JSONDecodeError, OSError):
            with contextlib.suppress(OSError):
                file_path.unlink(missing_ok=True)
            return None

        expires_at = data.get("expires_at")
        if expires_at is not None and time.time() > expires_at:
            with contextlib.suppress(OSError):
                file_path.unlink(missing_ok=True)
            return None

        return data.get("value")

Store a value in the cache as a JSON file.

Uses an atomic write (temp file + os.replace) so that concurrent readers in other processes always see a complete file — never a partial write. os.replace() maps to rename(2) on POSIX, which is guaranteed atomic for paths on the same filesystem.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default key str

Cache key.

required value Any

required ttl int | None

Time-to-live in seconds. If None, uses default_ttl. If both are None, the entry never expires.

None Source code in src/wordbase/cache.py 
def set(self, key: str, value: Any, ttl: int | None = None) -> None:
    """Store a value in the cache as a JSON file.

    Uses an atomic write (temp file + os.replace) so that concurrent
    readers in other processes always see a complete file — never a
    partial write. os.replace() maps to rename(2) on POSIX, which is
    guaranteed atomic for paths on the same filesystem.

    Args:
        key: Cache key.
        value: Value to cache (must be JSON-serializable).
        ttl: Time-to-live in seconds. If None, uses default_ttl.
            If both are None, the entry never expires.
    """
    effective_ttl = ttl if ttl is not None else self.default_ttl
    expires_at = time.time() + effective_ttl if effective_ttl else None
    content = json.dumps({"value": value, "expires_at": expires_at})
    file_path = self._key_to_path(key)
    with self._lock:
        fd, tmp = tempfile.mkstemp(dir=self.path, suffix=".tmp")
        try:
            with os.fdopen(fd, "w", encoding="utf-8") as fh:
                fh.write(content)
            os.replace(tmp, file_path)
        except OSError:
            with contextlib.suppress(OSError):
                os.unlink(tmp)
            raise

Source code in src/wordbase/cache.py 
def cleanup_expired(self) -> int:
    """Remove all expired entries from the cache directory.

    Scans every JSON file and deletes those whose TTL has elapsed.
    Files that cannot be read or parsed are silently skipped.

    Returns:
        Number of files deleted.
    """
    removed = 0
    now = time.time()
    cache_dir = Path(self.path)
    with self._lock:
        if not cache_dir.exists():
            return 0
        for entry in cache_dir.glob("*.json"):
            try:
                data = json.loads(entry.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
                expires_at = data.get("expires_at")
                if expires_at is not None and now > expires_at:
                    entry.unlink(missing_ok=True)
                    removed += 1
            except (json.JSONDecodeError, OSError):
                pass
    return removed

docs.title: wordbase.calibration
docs.text:
Calibration of label log-probability distributions.

Raw label log-probabilities produced by aggregate_logprobs/aggregate_counts are often over- or under-confident relative to the true label frequencies. This module provides:

  • CalibrationConfig: an immutable description of a fitted calibration transform (temperature scaling or Platt scaling).
  • apply_calibration: applies a CalibrationConfig to a label log-probability dict.
  • CalibrationFitter: fits a CalibrationConfig from held-out predictions and their true labels, optionally weighting each example by the effective sample size of its SampleUncertainty.

Typical usage example:

from wordbase.calibration import CalibrationFitter, apply_calibration

fitter = CalibrationFitter(method="temperature")
config = fitter.fit(predictions, true_labels)
calibrated = apply_calibration(predictions[0], config)

def fit(
    self,
    predictions: list[dict[T, float]],
    labels: list[T],
    *,
    uncertainties: list[SampleUncertainty[T] | None] | None = None,
) -> CalibrationConfig:
    """Fit a CalibrationConfig to minimise held-out negative log-likelihood.

    Args:
        predictions: One label log-probability dict per example, as
            produced by aggregate_logprobs/aggregate_counts.
        labels: The true label for each example, same length and order
            as predictions.
        uncertainties: Optional per-example SampleUncertainty objects
            (e.g. from aggregate_counts). When provided, each example is
            weighted by its uncertainty.n_samples, so low-variance
            estimates influence the fit more than high-variance ones.

    Returns:
        A fitted CalibrationConfig for self.method.

    Raises:
        ValueError: If predictions and labels have different lengths, or
            if uncertainties is provided with a different length.
    """
    if len(predictions) != len(labels):
        raise ValueError(
            "predictions and labels must have the same length "
            f"({len(predictions)} != {len(labels)})."
        )
    if uncertainties is not None and len(uncertainties) != len(predictions):
        raise ValueError(
            "uncertainties must have the same length as predictions "
            f"({len(uncertainties)} != {len(predictions)})."
        )

    weights = _resolve_weights(len(predictions), uncertainties)

    if self.method == "temperature":
        temperature = self._fit_temperature(predictions, labels, weights)
        return CalibrationConfig(method="temperature", temperature=temperature)

    platt_a, platt_b = self._fit_platt(predictions, labels, weights)
    return CalibrationConfig(method="platt", platt_a=platt_a, platt_b=platt_b)

def apply_calibration[T](
    logprobs: Mapping[T, float | torch.Tensor],  # noqa: F821
    config: CalibrationConfig,
) -> dict[T, float | torch.Tensor]:  # noqa: F821
    """Apply a calibration transform to a label log-probability dict.

    Values may be plain floats or torch.Tensor instances; the latter keep
    their computation graph intact through the transform and the
    renormalisation step (so gradients flow through when called with
    tensors produced by aggregate_logprobs).

    Args:
        logprobs: Dict mapping each label to its (normalised) log-probability,
            as produced by aggregate_logprobs/aggregate_counts.
        config: The calibration transform to apply.

    Returns:
        A dict with the same keys as logprobs, containing calibrated and
        renormalised log-probabilities. Returned unchanged when
        config.method == "none".

    Raises:
        CalibrationNotFittedError: If config.method is not "none" but the
            parameters required for that method have not been set.
    """
    if config.method == "none":
        return dict(logprobs)

    if config.method == "temperature":
        if config.temperature is None:
            raise CalibrationNotFittedError(
                "CalibrationConfig.temperature is None; call "
                "CalibrationFitter.fit() before applying temperature "
                "calibration."
            )
        scaled = {label: lp / config.temperature for label, lp in logprobs.items()}
        return _renormalize(scaled)

    if config.platt_a is None or config.platt_b is None:
        raise CalibrationNotFittedError(
            "CalibrationConfig.platt_a/platt_b are None; call "
            "CalibrationFitter.fit() before applying Platt calibration."
        )
    scaled = {
        label: config.platt_a * lp + config.platt_b for label, lp in logprobs.items()
    }
    return _renormalize(scaled)

docs.title: wordbase.classification
docs.text:
Standalone convenience functions for one-shot LLM classification.

These wrap the lower-level backend/LLMDistribution machinery for callers who want a single answer without constructing a reusable object:

  • surface_logprobs: raw, unnormalised log-probabilities for specific surface forms — useful for inspecting what a model assigns to candidate strings before committing to a full LLMDistribution.
  • classify: one-shot label classification, auto-building an identity Verbalizer when one is not supplied.

classify() returns a label -> log-probability dict (values <= 0, exp(v) sums to ~1 across labels) -- see wordbase.distribution for why log-space is used throughout.

Typical usage example:

from wordbase.classification import classify
from wordbase.providers.huggingface import HuggingFaceProvider

provider = HuggingFaceProvider("Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct")
log_probs = classify(provider, "Is the sky blue? Answer yes or no.",
                      labels=["yes", "no"])

def surface_logprobs(
    provider: LLMProvider,
    prompt: str,
    surfaces: list[str],
    *,
    top_k: int = 20,
    min_prob_floor: float = 1e-6,
) -> dict[str, float]:
    """Return raw log-probabilities for specific surface forms.

    Selects the most capable backend for provider and scores prompt against
    surfaces directly, with no Verbalizer aggregation or calibration
    applied. Useful for inspecting what a model assigns to candidate strings
    before committing to a full LLMDistribution.

    Args:
        provider: Any LLMProvider instance to query.
        prompt: The full prompt string to condition on.
        surfaces: Surface form strings to score.
        top_k: Top-k window requested from API logprob backends (forwarded
            to APILogprobBackend on auto-selection).
        min_prob_floor: Floor probability for surface forms outside the
            top-k window (forwarded to APILogprobBackend).

    Returns:
        Dict mapping each surface form to its log-probability (<= 0).

    Raises:
        NotImplementedError: If provider only supports sampling-based
            estimation (no backend exposes exact log-probabilities for it).
    """
    backend = select_backend(provider, top_k=top_k, min_prob_floor=min_prob_floor)
    if not isinstance(backend, LogprobBackend):
        raise NotImplementedError(
            f"{type(provider).__name__} does not expose exact surface "
            "log-probabilities; it only supports sampling-based estimation. "
            "Use classify() instead, which supports both the logprob and "
            "sampling paths."
        )
    return backend.surface_logprobs(prompt, surfaces)

def classify(
    provider: LLMProvider,
    prompt: str,
    labels: list[T],
    verbalizer: Verbalizer[T] | None = None,
    *,
    calibration: CalibrationConfig | None = None,
    n_samples: int | None = None,
    alpha: float | dict[T, float] = 1.0,
) -> dict[T, float]:
    """Classify a prompt against a set of labels in one call.

    Builds Verbalizer.identity(labels) when verbalizer is not supplied,
    constructs an LLMDistribution, and runs a single query(). Works with
    both the exact logprob path and the Monte Carlo sampling path,
    depending on what the provider supports.

    Args:
        provider: Any LLMProvider instance to query.
        prompt: The full prompt string to classify.
        labels: The candidate labels to classify prompt against.
        verbalizer: Maps labels to surface form strings. Defaults to
            Verbalizer.identity(labels) (each label's str() form is its own
            surface form).
        calibration: Calibration transform applied to the result. Defaults
            to an uncalibrated (method="none") config.
        n_samples: Sample count for the sampling path. None enables
            adaptive stopping.
        alpha: Dirichlet prior for the sampling path: a scalar applies a
            symmetric prior; a dict applies a per-label prior.

    Returns:
        Dict mapping each label to its calibrated log-probability (values
        <= 0, exp(v) sums to ~1 across labels).
    """
    if verbalizer is None:
        verbalizer = Verbalizer.identity(labels)

    distribution: LLMDistribution[T] = LLMDistribution(
        provider,
        verbalizer=verbalizer,
        calibration=calibration,
        n_samples=n_samples,
        alpha=alpha,
    )
    return cast(dict[T, float], distribution.query(prompt))

docs.title: wordbase.distribution
docs.text:
LLMDistribution: the public entry point for LLM-based classification.

LLMDistribution wraps an LLMProvider, a Verbalizer, and a CalibrationConfig behind a single query() interface that returns a normalised label distribution. It routes to either the exact logprob path or the Monte Carlo sampling path depending on the selected backend, but contains no routing logic itself — that lives entirely in the backend adapter classes (wordbase.backends). LLMDistribution only calls the LLMBackend protocol.

Every query() result is a label -> log-probability dict (values <= 0, exp(v) sums to ~1 across labels), regardless of which backend answered it -- log-space throughout is more numerically stable than renormalising linear probabilities, particularly for calibration's temperature/Platt scaling.

Typical usage example:

from wordbase import HuggingFaceProvider, LLMDistribution, Verbalizer

provider = HuggingFaceProvider("Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct")
verbalizer = Verbalizer({True: ["yes"], False: ["no"]})
dist = LLMDistribution(provider, verbalizer=verbalizer)

log_probs = dist.query("Is the sky blue? Answer yes or no.")
print(log_probs)  # {True: -0.02, False: -3.91}

def __init__(
    self,
    provider: LLMProvider,
    verbalizer: Verbalizer[T] | None = None,
    backend: LLMBackend | None = None,
    calibration: CalibrationConfig | None = None,
    prompt_template: str | Callable[[Any], str] = "{input}",
    n_samples: int | None = None,
    target_precision: float = 0.05,
    max_samples: int = 1000,
    max_unknown_fraction: float = 0.2,
    top_k_logprobs: int = 20,
    min_prob_floor: float = 1e-6,
    alpha: float | dict[T, float] = 1.0,
) -> None:
    """Construct an LLMDistribution.

    Args:
        provider: Any LLMProvider instance to query.
        verbalizer: Maps labels to surface form strings. Required unless
            backend is (or auto-selects to) HFClassifierBackend.
        backend: An explicit LLMBackend to use instead of auto-selection.
            Must have been constructed with this same provider instance.
        calibration: Calibration transform applied to every query()
            result. Defaults to an uncalibrated (method="none") config.
        prompt_template: Either a "{input}"-style format string (input is
            substituted via str.format(input=str(input))) or a callable
            that takes the input value and returns a prompt string.
        n_samples: Default sample count for the sampling path. None
            enables adaptive stopping.
        target_precision: 95% credible-interval half-width threshold for
            adaptive stopping (sampling path only).
        max_samples: Hard cap on samples for the sampling path.
        max_unknown_fraction: Fraction of unrecognised responses above
            which HighUnknownFractionWarning is raised (sampling path).
        top_k_logprobs: Top-k window requested from API logprob backends
            (forwarded to APILogprobBackend on auto-selection).
        min_prob_floor: Floor probability for surface forms outside the
            top-k window (forwarded to APILogprobBackend).
        alpha: Dirichlet prior for the sampling path: a scalar applies a
            symmetric prior; a dict applies a per-label prior.

    Raises:
        BackendMismatchError: If an explicit backend was constructed with
            a different provider than the one passed here.
        ValueError: If verbalizer is None and the resolved backend is not
            HFClassifierBackend.
    """
    self.provider = provider
    self.verbalizer = verbalizer
    self.calibration = (
        calibration if calibration is not None else CalibrationConfig()
    )
    self.prompt_template = prompt_template
    self.n_samples = n_samples
    self.target_precision = target_precision
    self.max_samples = max_samples
    self.max_unknown_fraction = max_unknown_fraction
    self.top_k_logprobs = top_k_logprobs
    self.min_prob_floor = min_prob_floor
    self.alpha = alpha
    self.last_uncertainty: SampleUncertainty[T] | None = None

    if backend is not None:
        if getattr(backend, "_provider", None) is not provider:
            raise BackendMismatchError(
                "The explicit backend was constructed with a different "
                "provider than the one passed to LLMDistribution."
            )
        self._backend = backend
    else:
        self._backend = select_backend(
            provider,
            top_k=top_k_logprobs,
            min_prob_floor=min_prob_floor,
            n=n_samples,
            target_precision=target_precision,
            max_samples=max_samples,
            alpha=alpha,
            max_unknown_fraction=max_unknown_fraction,
        )

    if verbalizer is None and not isinstance(self._backend, HFClassifierBackend):
        raise ValueError(
            "verbalizer is required unless the resolved backend is "
            "HFClassifierBackend (whose output is already a label "
            "distribution)."
        )

def query(  # pylint: disable=redefined-builtin
    self, input: Any, *, return_tensors: bool = False
) -> dict[T, float | torch.Tensor]:
    """Query the provider and return a calibrated label log-prob distribution.

    Args:
        input: The value to format into a prompt via prompt_template.
        return_tensors: When True, values are torch.Tensor with an
            attached computation graph instead of plain floats.

    Returns:
        Dict mapping each label to its calibrated log-probability
        (values <= 0, exp(v) sums to ~1 across labels; a torch.Tensor
        with an attached computation graph when return_tensors=True).
        This is log-space consistently, regardless of which backend
        answered the query -- including the sampling path, whose
        underlying posterior mean is a linear probability
        (SampleUncertainty.mean()) and is log-transformed here before
        calibration, since CalibrationConfig's temperature/Platt math
        operates in log-space.

    Raises:
        NotImplementedError: If return_tensors=True but the backend is
            not HFLogitBackend, or the backend is a sampling backend.
    """
    self.last_uncertainty = None
    prompt = self._format_prompt(input)

    if isinstance(self._backend, SamplingBackendBase):
        if return_tensors:
            raise NotImplementedError(
                "return_tensors=True is not supported for sampling backends."
            )
        assert self.verbalizer is not None  # enforced at construction time
        surfaces = self.verbalizer.all_surfaces()
        counts = self._backend.surface_counts(
            prompt,
            surfaces,
            self.n_samples,
            target_precision=self.target_precision,
            max_samples=self.max_samples,
        )
        weights, uncertainty = aggregate_counts(counts, self.verbalizer, self.alpha)
        self.last_uncertainty = uncertainty
        log_weights = {label: math.log(weight) for label, weight in weights.items()}
        return apply_calibration(log_weights, self.calibration)

    if return_tensors and not isinstance(self._backend, HFLogitBackend):
        raise NotImplementedError(
            "return_tensors=True is only supported when the backend is "
            "HFLogitBackend."
        )

    assert isinstance(self._backend, LogprobBackend)
    logprobs: dict[T, float | torch.Tensor]

    if isinstance(self._backend, HFClassifierBackend):
        # The classifier's own output IS the label distribution; the
        # Verbalizer (if any) plays no role in the classifier path.
        logprobs = cast(
            dict[T, Union[float, "torch.Tensor"]],
            self._backend.surface_logprobs(prompt, self._backend.labels),
        )
    else:
        assert self.verbalizer is not None  # enforced at construction time
        surfaces = self.verbalizer.all_surfaces()
        raw: dict[str, float | torch.Tensor]
        if return_tensors:
            assert isinstance(self._backend, HFLogitBackend)
            raw = self._backend.surface_logprobs(
                prompt, surfaces, return_tensors=True
            )
        else:
            raw = cast(
                dict[str, Union[float, "torch.Tensor"]],
                self._backend.surface_logprobs(prompt, surfaces),
            )
        logprobs = aggregate_logprobs(raw, self.verbalizer)

    return apply_calibration(logprobs, self.calibration)

def query_batch(
    self, inputs: list[Any], *, return_tensors: bool = False
) -> list[dict[T, float | torch.Tensor]]:
    """Query the provider for a batch of inputs.

    Each input is queried independently via query(); the sampling path
    maintains no shared state across inputs.

    Args:
        inputs: The values to format into prompts and query.
        return_tensors: Propagated to each individual query() call.

    Returns:
        List of calibrated label log-probability distributions (see
        query()'s Returns), one per input, in order.
    """
    return [self.query(item, return_tensors=return_tensors) for item in inputs]

    """Fit and install a calibration transform from held-out examples.

        texts: Input values to query, formatted via prompt_template.
        labels: The true label for each text, same length and order.
        method: Which calibration family to fit: "temperature" or
            "platt". Defaults to "temperature".
    """
    predictions: list[dict[T, float]] = []
    uncertainties: list[SampleUncertainty[T] | None] = []
    for text in texts:
        # fit_calibration never requests return_tensors, so query()
        # always returns plain floats here.
        predictions.append(cast(dict[T, float], self.query(text)))
        uncertainties.append(self.last_uncertainty)

    fitter: CalibrationFitter[T] = CalibrationFitter(method=method)
    has_uncertainty = any(u is not None for u in uncertainties)
    self.calibration = fitter.fit(
        predictions,
        labels,
        uncertainties=uncertainties if has_uncertainty else None,
    )

Custom exceptions and warnings for WordBase.

This module defines the exception and warning hierarchy for the WordBase library. All WordBase exceptions inherit from WordBaseError; all WordBase warnings inherit from the appropriate standard warning base class.

Exception Hierarchy

WordBaseError (base) ├── AuthenticationError ├── RateLimitError ├── ModelNotFoundError ├── InvalidRequestError ├── APIError ├── AmbiguousVerbalizerError ├── NoSurfaceMatchError ├── BackendMismatchError └── CalibrationNotFittedError

Warning Hierarchy

UserWarning (base) ├── HighUnknownFractionWarning ├── TopKTruncationWarning └── MultiTokenSurfaceWarning

Typical usage example:

from wordbase.exceptions import AuthenticationError

try:
    provider = OpenAIProvider(api_key="invalid")
except AuthenticationError as e:
    print(f"Authentication failed: {e}")

Issued when a surface form tokenizes to more than one token.

Teacher-forcing (extra API calls or forward passes) is used to score multi-token continuations, which may be slower and less accurate.

docs.title: wordbase.provider
docs.text:
Abstract base class for LLM providers.

This module defines the LLMProvider abstract base class that all provider implementations must inherit from. The provider interface ensures a consistent API across different LLM backends (HuggingFace, OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.).

Key responsibilities of providers
  • Convert between WordBase's internal message format and provider APIs
  • Make API calls or run local inference
  • Format responses into WordBase's standard completion format
  • Handle caching of responses
  • Support batch processing

Typical usage example:

from wordbase.providers.huggingface import HuggingFaceProvider
from wordbase.types import Message
from wordbase.cache import MemoryCache

provider = HuggingFaceProvider(model_name="gpt2")
provider.set_cache(MemoryCache())

messages = [Message(role="user", content="Hello")]
completions = provider.generate(messages, temperature=0.7)

docs.title: wordbase.types
docs.text:
Type definitions for WordBase messages.

This module defines the data structures used for representing conversations in WordBase. It provides a unified message format that works across all LLM providers, with support for both simple text and multimodal (text + image) content.

The module includes
  • TextContent: Represents a text content block
  • ImageContent: Represents an image content block
  • Message: Represents a single message in a conversation
  • Utility functions for converting between dicts and Message objects

Typical usage example:

from wordbase.types import Message, TextContent, ImageContent

# Simple text message
msg1 = Message(role="user", content="Hello")

# Multimodal message with text and image
msg2 = Message(
    role="user",
    content=[
        TextContent(type="text", text="What's in this image?"),
        ImageContent(type="image", source="path/to/image.jpg")
    ]
)

# Convert from dictionary
msg3 = Message.from_dict({"role": "assistant", "content": "Hi there!"})

@classmethod
def from_dict(cls, data: dict[str, Any]) -> "Message":
    """Create a Message from a dictionary.

    Args:
        data: Dictionary with 'role' and 'content' keys.
              Content can be a string or a list of content block dicts.

    Returns:
        Message object.

    Raises:
        KeyError: If required keys are missing.
        ValueError: If role or content block types are invalid.
        TypeError: If data structure is invalid.
    """
    role = data["role"]
    content_raw = data["content"]

    # Validate role
    if role not in ("system", "user", "assistant"):
        raise ValueError(
            f"Invalid role: {role}. Must be 'system', 'user', or 'assistant'."
        )

    # Handle string content
    if isinstance(content_raw, str):
        return cls(role=role, content=content_raw)

    # Handle list of content blocks
    if isinstance(content_raw, list):
        content_blocks: list[ContentBlock] = []
        for block in content_raw:
            if not isinstance(block, dict):
                raise TypeError(f"Content block must be a dict, got {type(block)}")

            block_type = block.get("type")

            if block_type == "text":
                content_blocks.append(TextContent(type="text", text=block["text"]))
            elif block_type == "image":
                content_blocks.append(
                    ImageContent(type="image", source=block["source"])
                )
            else:
                raise ValueError(f"Invalid content block type: {block_type}")

        return cls(role=role, content=content_blocks)

    raise TypeError(f"Content must be str or list, got {type(content_raw)}")

def to_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Convert Message to a dictionary.

    Returns:
        Dictionary representation of the message.
    """
    # Handle string content
    if isinstance(self.content, str):
        return {"role": self.role, "content": self.content}

    # Handle list of content blocks
    content_list = []
    for block in self.content:
        if isinstance(block, TextContent):
            content_list.append({"type": "text", "text": block.text})
        elif isinstance(block, ImageContent):
            content_list.append({"type": "image", "source": block.source})

    return {"role": self.role, "content": content_list}

docs.title: wordbase.verbalizer
docs.text:
Verbalizer: mapping between labels and surface form strings.

A Verbalizer maps each label T to one or more surface form strings and provides a reverse lookup from surface form back to label. It is used by LLMDistribution to translate between the model's textual outputs and the typed labels used by application code.

Typical usage example:

from wordbase.verbalizer import Verbalizer, BOOL_VERBALIZER

v = Verbalizer({True: ["yes", "correct"], False: ["no", "incorrect"]})
print(v.label_for("yes"))     # True
print(v.label_for("YES"))     # True  (case-insensitive by default)
print(v.label_for("maybe"))   # None

print(BOOL_VERBALIZER.label_for("true"))  # True

Verbalizer(mapping: dict[T, list[str]], case_sensitive: bool = False)

Maps labels to surface form strings with optional case folding.

Each label may have one or more surface forms. A reverse lookup table is built at construction so that label_for() runs in O(1). Duplicate surface forms across different labels raise AmbiguousVerbalizerError at construction time so callers discover conflicts early.

Attributes:

Name Type Description _mapping dict[T, list[str]]

The original label-to-surfaces mapping.

_case_sensitive

Whether matching honours letter case.

_reverse dict[str, T]

Pre-built surface-to-label reverse lookup.

Construct a Verbalizer from a label-to-surfaces mapping.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default mapping dict[T, list[str]]

Dict mapping each label to a list of surface form strings. Each surface form must appear under exactly one label.

required case_sensitive bool

When False (default) surface forms are compared after lowercasing. When True the comparison is exact.

False

Raises:

Type Description AmbiguousVerbalizerError

If any surface form appears under more than one label.

Source code in src/wordbase/verbalizer.py 
def __init__(
    self,
    mapping: dict[T, list[str]],
    case_sensitive: bool = False,
) -> None:
    """Construct a Verbalizer from a label-to-surfaces mapping.

    Args:
        mapping: Dict mapping each label to a list of surface form
            strings.  Each surface form must appear under exactly one
            label.
        case_sensitive: When False (default) surface forms are compared
            after lowercasing.  When True the comparison is exact.

    Raises:
        AmbiguousVerbalizerError: If any surface form appears under more
            than one label.
    """
    self._mapping: dict[T, list[str]] = {k: list(v) for k, v in mapping.items()}
    self._case_sensitive = case_sensitive
    self._reverse: dict[str, T] = {}

    for label, surfaces in self._mapping.items():
        for surface in surfaces:
            key = surface if case_sensitive else surface.lower()
            if key in self._reverse:
                if self._reverse[key] != label:
                    raise AmbiguousVerbalizerError(
                        f"Surface form {surface!r} is mapped to more than one "
                        f"label ({self._reverse[key]!r} and {label!r}). "
                        "Each surface form must appear under exactly one label."
                    )
            else:
                self._reverse[key] = label

@classmethod
def from_enum(cls, enum_type: type[T]) -> Verbalizer[T]:
    """Create a Verbalizer from an Enum class.

    Each enum member maps to two surface forms: its value (as a string)
    and its name.

    Args:
        enum_type: An Enum subclass.  Members without a string-compatible
            value are represented by their name only.

    Returns:
        A new Verbalizer with mapping {member: [str(member.value),
        member.name]} for each member.
    """
    mapping: dict[T, list[str]] = {}
    for member in enum_type:  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
        surfaces = []
        value_str = str(member.value)
        name_str = member.name
        surfaces.append(value_str)
        if name_str != value_str:
            surfaces.append(name_str)
        mapping[member] = surfaces
    return cls(mapping)

docs.title: wordbase.providers.anthropic
docs.text:
Anthropic provider for Claude models.

This module implements the APIProvider interface for Anthropic's messages API. It supports all Claude models available through the messages endpoint.

The provider handles
  • Authentication via explicit API key or ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var
  • Message format conversion from WordBase internal format to Anthropic format
  • Multimodal (text + image) messages via Claude's vision-capable models
  • Token counting via Anthropic's token counting API
  • Thinking content block extraction
  • Inherited concurrent batch processing and caching from APIProvider
Note

Anthropic's API does not expose token log-probabilities. The 'tokens' list in every completion will always be empty. Requesting return_token_probs=True emits a UserWarning and has no other effect.

Typical usage example:

from wordbase.providers.anthropic import AnthropicProvider
from wordbase.types import Message

provider = AnthropicProvider(model="claude-sonnet-4-6")

messages = [Message(role="user", content="What is 2+2?")]
completions = provider.generate(messages, temperature=0.0)
print(completions[0]["text"])

def count_tokens(self, text: str) -> int:
    """Count tokens in text using Anthropic's token counting API.

    Makes a lightweight API call to Anthropic's token counting endpoint.
    The text is wrapped in a single user message for the count.

    Args:
        text: Text to tokenize and count.

    Returns:
        Number of tokens according to the model's tokenizer.
    """
    response = self._client.messages.count_tokens(
        model=self.model,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}],
    )
    return response.input_tokens

docs.title: wordbase.providers.bedrock
docs.text:
Amazon Bedrock provider for foundation models.

This module implements the APIProvider interface for Amazon Bedrock using the unified converse API. It supports any Bedrock-compatible model (Claude, Llama, Titan, Mistral, and others) through a single message format.

The provider handles
  • AWS credential resolution via boto3 (explicit args, env vars, config file, or IAM instance role — in that priority order)
  • Message format conversion from WordBase internal format to Bedrock format
  • Multimodal (text + image) messages via converse's image content blocks, for vision-capable model IDs
  • Character-based token count estimation with a UserWarning
  • Inherited concurrent batch processing and caching from APIProvider
Note

The Bedrock converse API does not expose per-token log-probabilities. The 'tokens' list in every completion will always be empty. Requesting return_token_probs=True emits a UserWarning and has no other effect.

Typical usage example:

from wordbase.providers.bedrock import BedrockProvider
from wordbase.types import Message

provider = BedrockProvider(model="anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-6")

messages = [Message(role="user", content="What is 2+2?")]
completions = provider.generate(messages, temperature=0.0)
print(completions[0]["text"])

def __init__(
    self,
    model: str,
    region: str = "us-east-1",
    aws_access_key_id: str | None = None,
    aws_secret_access_key: str | None = None,
    aws_session_token: str | None = None,
) -> None:
    """Initialize the Amazon Bedrock provider.

    Args:
        model: Bedrock model ID (e.g. "anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-6",
            "meta.llama3-8b-instruct-v1:0").
        region: AWS region name. Defaults to "us-east-1".
        aws_access_key_id: Explicit AWS access key ID. Falls back to the
            ``AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`` environment variable or other credential
            sources when omitted.

        aws_session_token: Optional AWS session token for temporary
            credentials (e.g. from STS AssumeRole).

    Raises:
        ImportError: If boto3 is not installed.
    """
    super().__init__()
    self.model = model
    self.region = region

def count_tokens(self, text: str) -> int:
    """Estimate token count using a character-based approximation.

    The Bedrock converse API does not provide a dedicated token counting
    endpoint. This method returns a rough estimate of 1 token per 4
    characters and emits a UserWarning.

    Args:
        text: Text to estimate token count for.

    Returns:
        Estimated number of tokens (at least 1).
    """
    warnings.warn(
        "BedrockProvider uses a character-based token count estimate "
        "(4 characters per token). For exact counts, use a "
        "model-specific tokenizer.",
        UserWarning,
        stacklevel=2,
    )
    return max(1, len(text) // 4)

This module implements the APIProvider interface for Google's Gemini API via the google-genai SDK. It targets the Gemini Developer API with API key authentication, covering models such as Gemini 2.0 Flash and Gemini 2.5 Pro.

The provider handles

  • Message format conversion from WordBase internal format to Gemini format
  • Multimodal (text + image) messages via Gemini's native vision support
  • Token counting via the Gemini count_tokens API

Note

The standard Gemini generation API does not expose per-token log-probabilities. The 'tokens' list in every completion will always be empty. Requesting return_token_probs=True emits a UserWarning and has no other effect.

from wordbase.providers.google import GoogleProvider

messages = [Message(role="user", content="What is 2+2?")]

docs.title: wordbase.providers.huggingface
docs.text:
HuggingFace provider for local and hosted models.

This module implements the LLMProvider interface for HuggingFace Transformers models. It supports both local inference and models hosted on the HuggingFace Hub, with features including device placement, quantization, and token probability extraction.

The provider handles
  • Loading models and tokenizers from HuggingFace Hub or local paths
  • Device management (CPU, CUDA, MPS, auto)
  • 8-bit quantization for memory efficiency
  • Chat template formatting or fallback manual formatting
  • Token-level probability extraction
  • Sampling strategies (greedy, nucleus, top-k, beam search)
  • Multimodal (text + image) input for vision-language models, when constructed with multimodal=True

Typical usage example:

from wordbase.providers.huggingface import HuggingFaceProvider
from wordbase.types import Message

provider = HuggingFaceProvider(
    model_name="gpt2",
    device="cuda",
    load_in_8bit=True
)

messages = [Message(role="user", content="Hello, how are you?")]
completions = provider.generate(
    messages,
    temperature=0.7,
    max_tokens=100,
    return_token_probs=True
)

def __init__(
    self,
    model_name: str,
    device: str = "auto",
    load_in_8bit: bool = False,
    multimodal: bool = False,
    **model_kwargs: Any,
):
    """Initialize HuggingFace provider.

    Args:
        model_name: HuggingFace model identifier or local path.
        device: Device placement ("auto", "cuda", "cpu", "mps").
        load_in_8bit: Use 8-bit quantization.
        multimodal: Load a vision-language model (AutoProcessor +
            AutoModelForImageTextToText) instead of a text-only model.
        **model_kwargs: Additional arguments for
            AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained() (or
            AutoModelForImageTextToText.from_pretrained() when
            multimodal=True).

    Raises:
        ModelNotFoundError: If the model cannot be loaded.
    """
    super().__init__()

    self.model_name = model_name
    self.device = device
    self.load_in_8bit = load_in_8bit
    self.multimodal = multimodal
    self._processor: Any = None

    # Prepare model loading kwargs
    load_kwargs = {"device_map": device, **model_kwargs}

    if load_in_8bit:
        load_kwargs["load_in_8bit"] = True

    # MPS on macOS < 14 lacks support for several ops used during
    # generation (e.g. torch.isin on Long tensors, BFloat16). Fall back
    # to CPU in that case.
    if sys.platform == "darwin" and device in ("mps", "auto"):
        if torch.backends.mps.is_available():
            mac_ver = tuple(int(x) for x in platform.mac_ver()[0].split(".")[:2])
            if mac_ver < (14, 0):
                load_kwargs["device_map"] = "cpu"

    # Load model and tokenizer (or processor, for vision-language models)
    try:
        if multimodal:
            self._processor = AutoProcessor.from_pretrained(model_name)
            self.tokenizer = self._processor.tokenizer
            self.model = AutoModelForImageTextToText.from_pretrained(
                model_name, **load_kwargs
            )
        else:
            self.tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
            self.model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
                model_name, **load_kwargs
            )
    except Exception as e:
        raise ModelNotFoundError(
            f"Failed to load model '{model_name}': {str(e)}"
        ) from e

    # Set padding token if not set (needed for batching).
    # pad_token should be different from eos_token for proper masking.
    pad_token_id = getattr(self.tokenizer, "pad_token_id", None)
    eos_token_id = getattr(self.tokenizer, "eos_token_id", None)
    if self.tokenizer.pad_token is None or pad_token_id == eos_token_id:
        # Try to use unknown token first
        unk_token = getattr(self.tokenizer, "unk_token", None)
        unk_token_id = getattr(self.tokenizer, "unk_token_id", None)
        if unk_token is not None and unk_token_id != eos_token_id:
            self.tokenizer.pad_token = unk_token
        else:
            # Add a new pad token
            self.tokenizer.add_special_tokens({"pad_token": "<|pad|>"})
            # Resize model embeddings to accommodate new token
            self.model.resize_token_embeddings(len(self.tokenizer))
